query: can HIV point-of-care tests be used on sputum for detection?

retrieved_docs.title: Sputum Analysis -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Sputum Nucleic Acid Amplification Test
retrieved_docs.content: The result is positive if the RT-PCR amplification is successful. However, if it is not successful, the result is negative.

retrieved_docs.title: Sputum Analysis -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical diagnostic sputum tests are designed to identify the causes of lower respiratory tract infections and other diseases, making them a valuable tool for assessing the effectiveness of clinical treatments. Sputum culture is the most commonly performed test when a patient is diagnosed with pneumonia, as it helps identify the bacteria or fungi causing the airway or lung infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Sputum Analysis -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: The following procedures outline the diagnostic techniques used to analyze sputum samples for identifying pathogens, assessing cellular abnormalities, and determining antimicrobial susceptibility. These tests are crucial for diagnosing a variety of respiratory conditions, including infections and lung diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Screening Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Of note, 4th generation assays can produce a false positive Ag/Ab, which is known to be false by the lack of both HIV 1/2Ab and a confirmatory RNA test. If the first step, Ag/Ab is positive, the test must be completed in its entirety with an HIV 1/2Ab and RNA test that results in a positive or negative final result. In the scenario of only partial results from a 4th-generation test (due to an insufficient sample), the results are not valid. See Figure 1.[CDC Stacks. 2018 Quick reference guide: Recommended laboratory HIV testing algorithm for serum or plasma specimens ]. Point-of-care testing for HIV continues to evolve in clinical practice. Currently, there is one single-use, rapid point-of-care assay capable of detecting both HIV-1 p24 antigen and antibodies to HIV-1/2. This test, performed with a fingerstick specimen, is CLIA-waived and identifies HIV infection earlier than antibody-only rapid tests. However, its sensitivity during early infection remains lower than that of the recommended laboratory-based, fourth-generation serum HIV screen. While several rapid antibody-only point-of-care tests are available, they are not routinely recommended in clinical care. Nonetheless, these tests may be valuable in specific circumstances, such as occupational exposures or when women present in labor without documented prenatal HIV screening. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Molecular detection methods
retrieved_docs.content: The past 2 decades have brought a remarkable evolution in the approach to TB diagnosis and drug-resistance detection. Molecular methods using NAATs and lateral flow biomarker detection provide rapid results that immediately impact patient care and public health. Several of these tests are capable of simultaneous tuberculosis detection and drug resistance identification. [12] The application of these tests continues to evolve; when, where, and how they are best utilized requires consideration of multiple variables. These variables include the following: Prevalence of TB and drug resistance Purpose of testing, eg, population screening, individual diagnosis of active pulmonary or extrapulmonary TB Patient population, eg, adults, children, or PLHIV Testing context, eg, stand-alone or follow-on testing Costs Ease of use Available resources Pretest probability, sensitivity, and specificity Interpretation when discordant with conventional methods

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Screening Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: The 4th-generation HIV test offers significant advantages over earlier-generation assays, primarily due to its ability to detect infection at an earlier stage and to differentiate between HIV-1 and HIV-2—an important distinction with therapeutic implications. In the Vaginal and Oral Interventions to Control the Epidemic (VOICE) study, 4th-generation testing identified 28% of HIV infections that were missed by 3rd-generation tests.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure -- Sputum Specimen
retrieved_docs.content: For certain diseases like mycobacterium tuberculosis ( M. tuberculosis ), a series of 3 early morning specimens, 8-10 mL in quantity, are collected and stored in a refrigerator. In uncooperative patients, M. tuberculosis can also be recovered from gastric contents by stomach aspiration.

retrieved_docs.title: Sputum Analysis -- Testing Procedures -- Sputum Biochemical Test Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteria are first inoculated into a series of differential media to identify the suspected organism. Various indicators are then used to observe the specific end products of metabolism inside the medium.

retrieved_docs.title: Sputum Analysis -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory viruses can be detected in sputum specimens from patients with cystic fibrosis, asthma, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. [3] [4] [5] Viral testing is typically performed on upper airway samples such as nasopharyngeal swabs or nasal washes. However, some viral pathogens, such as SARS-CoV, H1N1 influenza, Middle Eastern respiratory syndrome coronavirus, and SARS-CoV-2, the causative agent of COVID-19, may be absent in upper airway secretions. [6] [7] [8] [9] Consequently, sputum samples are frequently used for viral diagnosis testing using real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) or the newly developed next-generation sequencing method. Potentially, face masks, which reduce the aerosol-related risk of transmission in the current era of the COVID-19 pandemic, may also represent a useful source for next-generation sequencing investigations. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Screening Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of suspected acute HIV infection occurring fewer than 14 days after exposure, an HIV RNA test should be performed as the initial diagnostic test. Depending on the sensitivity of the RNA assay, viral RNA may be detectable as early as 5 to 10 days posttransmission. HIV RNA testing is also warranted when 4th-generation test results are indeterminate.

retrieved_docs.title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Selecting Specific Diagnostic Testing for Genitourinary Tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mycobacterium tuberculosis culture is considered definitive, but a final test result can take from 6 to 8 weeks; this is best used together with other, quicker testing modalities, such as polymerase chain reaction. [21] At least 3 consecutive morning samples are recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Screening Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: In nonclinical settings, oral swab tests continue to be primarily based on rapid antibody tests based on enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) technology, performed as rapid point-of-care diagnostics. Positive results must be confirmed with a serum-based Western blot. A key advantage of rapid tests is their applicability in settings outside traditional healthcare facilities, such as community health fairs, faith-based organizations, and HIV service centers. Results are typically available within 20 minutes, minimizing the risk of patients failing to receive their results due to loss to follow-up. ELISA tests detect only antibodies and can identify infection approximately 3 weeks after transmission. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Initial diagnostic testing of TB with drug-resistance testing
retrieved_docs.content: The sensitivity of Xpert and Xpert Ultra assays in detecting pulmonary TB in children is lower than in adults, likely due to the difficulty of obtaining adequate sputum samples in children. Both Xpert and Xpert Ultra assays are valuable in detecting tuberculosis from samples obtained at extrapulmonary sites, most notably cerebrospinal fluid and lymph node aspirates. The WHO 2021 update document details the sensitivity and specificity of the test for various extrapulmonary specimens. The WHO recommends using Xpert or Xpert Ultra assays as initial tests in adults and children, including PLHIV, with signs and symptoms of pulmonary TB. The tests can analyze a variety of specimens, including gastric or nasopharyngeal aspirates and stool specimens in children. The WHO also recommends their use as an initial diagnostic test in adults and children suspected of having extrapulmonary TB. For PLHIV suspected of having disseminated TB, the WHO recommends using the Xpert and Xpert Ultra assays on blood.

retrieved_docs.title: Point-of-Care Testing -- Diagnostic Tests -- Testing Strips and Lateral-flow Testing
retrieved_docs.content: In many instances, POCTs that utilize simple test strips or lateral-flow testing provide qualitative or semiquantitative results and do not provide precise information regarding the specific concentration of the measured analyte.

retrieved_docs.title: Point-of-Care Testing -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Various guidelines, such as the ASSURED Guidelines by the World Health Organization (WHO), exist for specific subsets of POCT, such as sexually transmitted infections (STI). [9] The ASSURED acronym stands for Affordable, Sensitive, Specific, User-friendly, Rapid, Robust, Equipment-free, and Delivered (to the end user), which are key criteria for effective POCT, as proposed by the World Health Organization (WHO). Affordable is for patients at risk of infection,  and equipment-free means no complex equipment is required.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A negative HIV-1 nucleic acid amplification test result in the last settings indicates a false-positive HIV-1 test. When clinical suspicion for HIV is high and initial test results are negative, testing should be repeated in 1 to 3 weeks. Home-based, point-of-care, or rapid tests are essential to increase testing frequency or extend testing into populations that may otherwise not get tested. As with other HIV tests, positive results from point-of-care testing should be followed with standard laboratory, instrument-based immunoassays to confirm the diagnosis. [22]

Consequently, leukopenia serves as a common clinical link between this symptom complex and HIV infection, indicating that patients presenting with low white‑blood‑cell counts should be evaluated for underlying HIV disease and related symptomatology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2579
retrieved_docs.content: A positive HIV test result (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي) is clinically associated with leukopenia (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض). Leukopenia is also classified as a member of the drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms syndrome (متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ)). This connection indicates that a low white‑blood‑cell count can arise both from HIV infection and as a component of severe drug‑induced hypersensitivity reactions, emphasizing the need for careful monitoring of white‑blood‑cell levels in patients with HIV or those exposed to medications that may trigger this syndrome.

Bacterial pneumonia possesses a transmissible component (přenos), underscoring the need for infection‑control measures when managing affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1166
retrieved_docs.content: Stomach cancer, referred to as “cancer of stomach” and also named سرطان المعدة in Arabic, is clinically associated with a low white‑blood‑cell count (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض). This leukopenia is further linked to a broader SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX and is commonly observed in patients who have a positive HIV test (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي). Consequently, leukopenia serves as a shared clinical finding that connects gastrointestinal malignancy, HIV infection, and a constellation of signs and symptoms, underscoring its importance for diagnosis, monitoring, and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Infectious Disease Terminology incorporates فحص التوبركولين (the tuberculin test) as a specific concept within its scope. فحص التوبركولين is classified as a type of immunizations and screening for infectious disease, highlighting its function in the detection of latent tuberculosis infection. Immunizations and screening for infectious disease serves as a broader category that also encompasses Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings, which are organized under this same parent heading. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how the tuberculin test and various nonspecific laboratory abnormalities are situated within the overarching framework of infectious disease screening and immunization practices, with the pediatric terminology providing a focused context for the tuberculin test.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1952
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified under the broader category الْتِهابِ شعبي. Clinically, acute bronchitis is associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ. The condition الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ includes a subcategory called التهاب رئوي وليدي. Both الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ and the measurement حج القحف are linked to a positive فحص التوبركولين, indicating that pulmonary inflammation and head circumference may be related to tuberculin reactivity. This network of relationships highlights the hierarchical classification of respiratory inflammatory disorders and their potential association with immunologic testing results.
